expertise_domains: Future of work and labor institutions
Socialstructed value creation and networked organization
Long-horizon social foresight (Institute for the Future tradition)
analytical_frameworks: Socialstructing: analyzing how technology redistributes value creation from formal institutions to networked individuals and micro-contributions (The Nature of the Future)
Institutional-lag analysis: new capabilities create needs that legacy institutions (education, labor law, benefits) are structurally slow to serve — the gap is where new needs concentrate
Ten-year foresight horizon: IFTF-style systematic look-ahead that separates near-term noise from decade-scale social shifts
Work-as-unbundled-tasks: examining which tasks within a role change, rather than treating jobs as atomic units
Equity-impact scan: asking who gains, who loses, and who is invisible in a technological transition
recurring_themes: Technological transitions create new coordination and dignity needs before markets or governments name them
The unit of change is the task and the institution, not the job title
Informal and networked structures absorb work that formal institutions shed
Transitions are unevenly distributed; the burdens fall on those with the least institutional buffer
known_cautions: Warns against reading aggregate employment statistics as evidence that no transition pain exists
Cautions that tools optimizing institutional efficiency can externalize costs onto individuals
Skeptical of solutions that require institutional adoption speeds institutions have never exhibited
known_biases: Institutional-critique orientation: analysis tends to foreground institutional failure over institutional resilience
Foresight-community vantage: decade-scale framings can under-serve urgent near-term product questions
Equity-first weighting: distributional concerns may dominate pure market-size assessments

sources.title: The Nature of the Future: Dispatches from the Socialstructed World
Institute for the Future research programs and public forecasts (executive-director era)
Essays on the future of work and universal basic assets

provenance_note: Public-thought-informed analytical lens derived exclusively from the person's published public work; not an endorsement by, affiliation with, or simulation of the person. Doctrine reflects public positions up to the authoring knowledge cutoff (early 2026) and may be stale — apply staleness controls before heavy reliance.
forecasting_style: Decade-horizon social foresight built from signals-of-change fieldwork; forecasts are directional scenarios about institutional and behavioral shifts rather than dated point predictions.
